pages.title: Application to the Picard group
pages.html:
XI Application to the Picard group

This exposé is modelled on the preceding one, but this time the result of n° 1 is less strong.

Throughout this exposé, \(X\) will denote a locally noetherian prescheme, \(\mathscr{I}\) a quasi-coherent ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_X\), (so \(Y=V(\mathscr{I})\) is a closed part of \(X\)), \(U\) a variable open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), and \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\). For every ringed space \((Z, \mathscr{O}_Z)\), we denote by \(\mathbf{P}(Z)\) the category of invertible \(\mathscr{O}_Z\)-Modules, in other words those that are locally free of rank \(1\), and by \(\operatorname{Pic}(Z)\) the group of isomorphism classes of invertible Modules on \(Z\).

Throughout this exposé, \(X\) will denote a locally noetherian prescheme, \(\mathscr{I}\) a quasi-coherent ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_X\), (so \(Y=V(\mathscr{I})\) is a closed part of \(X\)), \(U\) a variable open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), and \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\). For every ringed space \((Z, \mathscr{O}_Z)\), we denote by \(\mathbf{P}(Z)\) the category of invertible \(\mathscr{O}_Z\)-Modules, in other words those that are locally free of rank \(1\), and by \(\operatorname{Pic}(Z)\) the group of isomorphism classes of invertible Modules on \(Z\).

pages.title: Comparison of \(\operatorname{Pic}(\widehat{X})\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\)
pages.html:
1 Comparison of \(\operatorname{Pic}(\widehat{X})\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\)

For every \(n\in\mathbf{N}\), put \(X_n = (Y, \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{n+1})\) and \(P_n = \mathscr{I}^{n+1}/\mathscr{I}^{n+2}\). The sequence of sheaves of abelian groups on \(Y\)

\[ \tag{1.1} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_n\xrightarrow{u}\mathscr{O}_{X_{n+1}}^*\xrightarrow{v}\mathscr{O}_{X_n}^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1 \]

is exact. Let us specify that the group structure on \(P_n\) is the additive structure, that \(u(x)=1+x\) for every \(x\in P_n\), and that \(v\) is the homomorphism deduced from the injection \(\mathscr{I}^{n+2}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\). One sees that \(v\) is surjective by remarking that, for every \(y \in Y\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X_n, y}\) is a local ring, a quotient of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{n+1}, y}\) by a nilpotent ideal; the rest is just as trivial. From (1.1) one deduces an exact cohomology sequence:

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{H}^1(Y, P_n)\xrightarrow{u^1}\operatorname{H}^1(Y, \mathscr{O}_{X_{n+1}}^*)\xrightarrow{v^1} \operatorname{H}^1(Y, \mathscr{O}_{X_n}^*)\xrightarrow{d}\operatorname{H}^2(Y, P_n){}. \]

Moreover, for every \(n\in\mathbf{N}\), one knows how to identify \(\operatorname{Pic}(X_n)\) and \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, \mathscr{O}_{X_n}^*)\); furthermore, if \(E\) is an invertible \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{n+1}}\)-Module, corresponding to a cohomology class \(c(E)\), the cohomology class corresponding to the inverse image of \(E\) on \(X_n\) is equal to \(v^1(c(E))\). Whence the following proposition:

Proposition 1.1

Let us keep the notations introduced above. Let \(p\in\mathbf{N}\). The map \(\operatorname{Pic}(\widehat{X})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(Y_n)\):

  1. is injective for \(n\geqslant p\), if \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, P_n)=0\) for \(n\geqslant p\);

  2. is an isomorphism for \(n\geqslant p\), if \(\operatorname{H}^i(Y, P_n)=0\) for \(n\geqslant p\) and \(i=1, 2\).

Of course, the exact sequence (*) contains more information than the proposition above. The reader will have remarked that nothing has been said about the functor \(\mathbf{P}(\widehat{X})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{P}(Y)\). Given two invertible \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Modules, \(H=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)\) is also invertible. If one indicates by a subscript \(n\) the reduction modulo \(\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\), one finds an exact sequence:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H_0\otimes P_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E_{n+1}, F_{n+1}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E_n, F_n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0{}. \]

Whence an exact cohomology sequence which we shall not write out and whose interpretation is evident; one may use this remark to study the functor \(\mathbf{P}\).

pages.title: Comparison of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(\widehat{X})\)
pages.html:
2 Comparison of \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(\widehat{X})\)

The reader will find in Exposé X, n° 2, the proof of the following:

Proposition 2.1

Suppose that \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\) holds; then for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), the functor

\[ \tag{2.1} \mathbf{P}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{P}(\widehat{X}) \]

is fully faithful, and the map

\[ \tag{2.2} \operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(\widehat{X}) \]

is therefore injective. If \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\) holds, the map (2.3) is an isomorphism:

\[ \tag{2.3} \varinjlim_U\operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(\widehat{X}){}. \]
Corollary 2.2

Suppose that \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\) holds and that for every integer \(n\geqslant p\) we have \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, P_n)=0\); then for every open set \(U\supset Y\), the maps

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y_n) \]

are injective if \(n\geqslant p\). If \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\) holds and if moreover, for every integer \(n\geqslant p\), we have \(\operatorname{H}^i(Y, P_n)=0\) for \(i=1\) and \(i=2\), then the map

\[ \varinjlim_U\operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y_n) \]

is an isomorphism for \(n\geqslant p\).

pages.title: Comparison of \(\mathbf{P}(X)\) and \(\mathbf{P}(U)\)
pages.html:
3 Comparison of \(\mathbf{P}(X)\) and \(\mathbf{P}(U)\)

A definition:

Definition 3.1

Let \(X\) be a prescheme and let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Set \(U=X-Z\). One says that \(X\) is parafactorial at the points of \(Z\) if, for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the functor \(\mathbf{P}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}(V\cap U)\) is an equivalence of categories. One also says that the pair \((X, Z)\) is parafactorial.

Recall that \(\mathbf{P}(Z)\) denotes the category of locally free Modules of rank \(1\) on \(Z\).

Definition 3.2

A noetherian local ring is said to be parafactorial if the pair \((\operatorname{Spec}(A), \{\mathfrak{r}(A)\})\) is parafactorial.

One proves the following proposition, which shows that the notion is "pointwise":

Proposition 3.3

Suppose \(X\) locally noetherian. In order that the pair \((X, Z)\) be parafactorial, it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(z\in Z\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, z}\) be so.

Let us note that within parafactorial there is "fully faithful". One proves, as in lemma 3.5 of exposé X, the:

Lemma 3.4

If \(X\) is a locally noetherian prescheme and if \(Z=X-U\) is a closed subset of \(X\), the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the functor \(\mathbf{P}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}(V\cap U)\) is fully faithful;

  2. the homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*(\mathscr{O}_U)\) is an isomorphism;

  3. for every \(z\in Z\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}(\mathscr{O}_{X, z})\geqslant 2\).

Thus "parafactorial" means that the conditions of 3.4 are satisfied and that, for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the homomorphism \(\operatorname{Pic}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(V\cap U)\), is surjective. In particular, if \(X\) is the spectrum of a noetherian local ring, one finds:

Proposition 3.5

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; in order for it to be parafactorial it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(X'-\{x\})=0\), where one has set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and where \(x\) is the unique closed point of \(X'\).

One will note that a local ring of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) is never parafactorial, since its depth is \(\leqslant 1\). Hence factorial does not entail parafactorial; this is however true for noetherian local rings of dimension \(\geqslant 2\), as we shall see below.

Lemma 3.6

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(f\colon X_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Set \(Z_1=f^{-1}(Z)\). If \((X_1, Z_1)\) is parafactorial, then \((X, Z)\) is so.

One first remarks that, if \(i\colon(X-Z)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) denotes the canonical immersion of \(U=X-Z\) into \(X\), the formation of the direct image by \(i\) of a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module commutes with the base change \(f\), since the latter is flat. It is therefore equivalent to assume the equivalent conditions of lemma 3.5 for \((X, Z)\) or for \((X_1, Z_1)\), since \(f\) is a descent morphism for the category of quasi-coherent sheaves. It remains to prove that, for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), \(\operatorname{Pic}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(V\cap U)\) is surjective. One performs the base change \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), which changes nothing (sic), and one is reduced to \(V=X\). One then remarks that, if \({L}\) is an invertible \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module and if \({L}\) admits a locally free embedding, this prolongation is isomorphic to \(i_*({L})\), because of what has just been seen. It remains to prove that \(i_*({L})\) is invertible. Using again the fact that the direct image by \(i\) commutes with flat base change, and that "locally free of rank \(1\)" is a property that descends by faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, we have won.

Corollary 3.7

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; if \(\widehat{A}\) is parafactorial then \(A\) is so.

Do not believe that, if \(A\) is parafactorial, \(\widehat{A}\) is so.

!

Before approaching the statement of the principal theorem of this , let us make the link with the theory of divisors and the notion of factorial ring1.

Let \(X\) be a noetherian and normal prescheme. Let \(Z^1(X)\) be the free abelian group generated by the \(x\in X\) such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}=1\). The local ring of such a point is a discrete valuation ring. One will denote by \(v_x\) the corresponding normalized valuation. Let \(K(X)\) be the ring of rational fractions of \(X\) and let

\[ p\colon K(X)^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^1(X) \]

be the map which to every \(f\in K(X)^*\) associates the codimension-1 cycle:

\[ (f) = \sum_{x\in X, \;\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}=1} v_x(f)\cdot x{}. \]

The image of \(p\) is denoted \(P(X)\) and one calls its elements principal divisors2. One sets

\[ \operatorname{Cl}(X) = Z^1(X)/P(X){}. \]

Let \(Z\) be the subgroup of \(Z^1(X)\) whose elements are the locally principal divisors. One knows that

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\simeq Z^{\prime1}(X)/P(X){}, \]

and consequently \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) is identified with a subgroup of \(\operatorname{Cl}(X)\).

Let us remark that if \(U\) is a dense open set of \(X\), \(K(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K(U)\) is an isomorphism, and that if \(\operatorname{codim}(X-U, X)\geqslant 2\), i.e. if every \(x\in X\) such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\leqslant 1\) belongs to \(U\), the homomorphism \(Z^1(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^1(U)\) and consequently \(\operatorname{Cl}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Cl}(U)\) is also an isomorphism. Finally, if every \(x\in U\) is factorial, i.e. \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is so, then \(Z^1(U) = Z^{\prime1}(U)\) hence \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\simeq\operatorname{Cl}(U)\).

Proposition 3.7.1

Let \(X\) be a noetherian and normal prescheme. Let \((U_i)_{i\in I}\) be a family of open sets of \(X\) such that:

  1. the \(U_i\) form a filter base;

  2. if one sets \(Y_i=X-U_i\), one has \(\operatorname{codim}(Y_i, X)\geqslant 2\) for every \(i\),

  3. if \(x\in U_i\) for every \(i\in I\), then \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is factorial.

Then one has an isomorphism:

\[ \varinjlim_{i\in I}\operatorname{Pic}(U_i)\xrightarrow{\approx}\operatorname{Cl}(X){}. \]

Let us remark that b) entails that every \(x\in X\) such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\leqslant 1\) belongs to \(U_i\) for every \(i\). Hence the \(U_i\) are dense and moreover the homomorphism \(Z^1(U_i)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^1(X)\) is an isomorphism, as is \(K(U_i)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K(X)\). Hence \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\subset \operatorname{Cl}(U_i) \simeq \operatorname{Cl}(X)\). To prove what one desires, it therefore suffices to show that every \(D\in Z^1(X)\) belongs to \(Z^{\prime1}(U_i)\) for a suitable \(i\). It suffices to do it for the irreducible and positive "divisors". So let \(x\in X\) be such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}=1\). It suffices to prove that there exists an \(i\in I\) such that \(\overline{\{x\}}\) is locally principal at the points of \(U_i\). Let \(\mathscr{I}\) be the largest ideal of definition of the closed set \(\overline{\{x\}}\). The set of points in a neighbourhood of which \(\mathscr{I}\) is free is an open set \(U\). Now \(U\supset \bigcap_{i\in I}U_i\) by c). If one sets \(Y=X-U\), one has \(Y\subset \bigcup_{i\in I}Y_i\), now \(Y\) is closed, hence admits a finite number of generic points, hence is contained in the union of a finite number of \(Y_i\), hence in \(Y_j\) for a \(j\in I\), since the \(U_i\) form a filter base. Hence \(U\supset U_j\).Q.E.D.

Corollary 3.8

Let \(X\) be a noetherian and normal prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed subset of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Suppose that, for every \(p\in X-Y\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X, p}\) is factorial, then

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X-Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Cl}(X-Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Cl}(X) \]

are isomorphisms.

Corollary 3.9

Let \(A\) be a noetherian and normal local ring. Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(x = \mathfrak{r}(A)\). In order that \(A\) be factorial it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X'-\{x\})=0\) and that \(\mathfrak{p}\in X'-\{x\}\) imply that \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) is factorial.

Indeed, in order that \(A\) be factorial it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{Cl}(X')=0\) .

Corollary 3.10

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 2\). Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and let \(x = \mathfrak{r}(A)\). Set \(X=X'-\{x\}\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(A\) is factorial;

  2. a) for every \(y\in X\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X, p}\) is factorial, and

    6.3mmb) \(A\) is parafactorial, i.e. \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=0\).

Before proving this corollary, let us state the

Serre's normality criterion 3.11

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. In order that \(A\) be normal, it is necessary and sufficient that

  1. for every prime ideal \({\mathfrak{p}}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\leqslant 1\), \(A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\) be normal,

  2. for every prime ideal \({\mathfrak{p}}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\geqslant 2\), one have \(\operatorname{prof} A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\geqslant 2\).

Let us prove 3.10.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Knowing that a localization of a factorial ring is also factorial, one has (ii) a). Moreover \(A\) is normal hence \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) since \(\dim A\geqslant 2\) (3.11 (ii)). Finally \(A\) is parafactorial; indeed \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\simeq\operatorname{Cl}(X')=0\) (cf. 3.9).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). Let us first prove that \(A\) is normal by applying SERRE's criterion. Since \(\dim A\geqslant 2\), condition (i) of the criterion is among the hypotheses. Moreover, for every \({\mathfrak{p}}\in X\), \(A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\) is factorial, hence normal, hence of depth \(\geqslant 2\), at least if \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\geqslant 2\). Finally \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) by (ii) b). It remains to apply 3.9.

Let us summarize what precedes:

Proposition 3.12

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(\mathscr{I}\) be a quasi-coherent ideal of \(X\). Let \(Y=V(\mathscr{I})\). Let \(p\in\mathbf{N}\). Suppose that:

  1. 1)

    One has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\) (Exposé X);

  2. 2)

    \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, \mathscr{I}^{n+1}/\mathscr{I}^{n+2})=0\) if \(i=1\) or \(2\) and if \(n\geqslant p\);

  3. 3)

    for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\) and every \(x\in X-U\), the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) be parafactorial.

Then, for every \(n\geqslant p\), and every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\), the homomorphisms

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathrm{Pic}(X)}\ar[rr]\ar[dr]&&{\mathrm{Pic}(X_n)}\\&{\mathrm{Pic}(U)}\ar[ur]&} \]

are isomorphisms.

One knows of some parafactorial rings:

Theorem 3.13

  1. (Auslander-Buchsbaum) A regular noetherian local ring is factorial (hence parafactorial if its dimension is \(\geqslant 2\)).

  2. A noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 4\) which is a complete intersection is parafactorial.

Corollary (SAMUEL's conjecture)

A noetherian local ring \(A\) which is a complete intersection and which is factorial in codimension \(\geqslant 3\) (i.e. \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\leqslant 3\) entails that \(A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\) is factorial) is factorial.

Let us prove the corollary

One argues by induction on the dimension of \(A\).

If \(\dim A\leqslant 3\), \(A\) is factorial by hypothesis.

If \(\dim A>3\), by the induction hypothesis, remarking that a localization of a complete intersection is also one, all the localizations of \(A\) other than \(A\) are factorial. By theorem 3.13 (ii), \(A\) is parafactorial, hence factorial by 3.10.

Let us prove 3.13 (i) (following KAPLANSKY)3.

Let \(A\) be a regular noetherian local ring, set \(\dim A=n\). If \(n=0\) or \(1\), the result is known. Suppose \(n\geqslant 2\), let us argue by induction on \(n\) and suppose \(n\geqslant 2\) and the theorem proved for rings of dimension \(<n\). Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=X'-\{x\}\), where \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\). The localizations of \(A\) other than \(A\) are regular and of dimension \(<n\), hence factorial. Moreover \(\operatorname{prof} A = \dim A\geqslant 2\). It therefore suffices to prove that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=0\) (cor. 3.10). So let \({L}\) be an invertible \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module; one knows that it can be prolonged to a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Module \({L}'\). There exists a resolution of \({L}'\) by free \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}'\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}'_1\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}\cdots\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{L}'_n\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} 0{}, \]

since the cohomological dimension of \(A\) is finite. By restriction to \(X'\) one obtains a finite free resolution. It therefore suffices to prove the following lemma:

Lemma 3.15

Let \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) be a ringed space and let \({L}\) be a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module which admits a finite resolution by free modules of finite type. Then \(\det({L}) \simeq \mathscr{O}_X\).

Recall that one defines \(\det({L})\) as the maximal exterior power of \({L}\). In the case considered, \(\det({L}) \simeq{L}\) since \({L}\) is invertible, hence the lemma allows one to conclude. Let us prove this lemma. Let

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}_0\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}_1\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}_2\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}\cdots\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{L}_n\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} 0{}, \]

be the announced exact sequence, where \({L}_0={L}\). Since everything is locally free, one has:

\[ \mathop{\textstyle\bigotimes}\limits_{0\leqslant i\leqslant n}(\det({L}_i))^{(-1)^i} \simeq \mathscr{O}_X \]

now all the \({L}_i\), \(i>0\), are free, hence so are their determinants, hence so is that of \({L}_0 = {L}\).Q.E.D.

It remains for us to prove part (ii) of the theorem. Beforehand, let us prove a lemma which will allow us to proceed by induction:

Lemma 3.16

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring that is a quotient of a regular one. Let \(t\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be an \(A\)-regular element. Suppose that \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology. Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(Y'=V(t)\simeq \operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(B=A/tA\), \(X=X'-\{x\}\), \(Y=Y'-\{x\}\), \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\). Suppose that:

  1. for every \(y\in X\) closed in \(X\) one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\geqslant 2\),

  2. \(\operatorname{prof} A/tA\geqslant 3\),

then the map \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is injective. In particular, if \(B\) is parafactorial, \(A\) is so too.

One knows that a) entails \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\) thanks to X 2.1. If one proves that \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, P_n)=0\) for every \(n\geqslant 0\), one will know (2.2) that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is injective. If, moreover, \(B\) is parafactorial, one will know that \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)=0\) (3.5) hence \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=0\), now \(\operatorname{prof}(A)=3+1\geqslant 2\) since \(t\) is \(A\)-regular, hence \(A\) will be parafactorial by 3.5.

Let \(\mathscr{I}=\widetilde{(tA)}\) be the \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Module associated with the ideal \(tA\). In n° 1, one set \(P_n = (\mathscr{I}^{n+1}/\mathscr{I}^{n+2}){|Y}\), for every \(n\geqslant 0\). Now \(t\) is \(A\)-regular hence \(P_n\simeq \mathscr{O}_Y\). It therefore remains for us to prove that \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, \mathscr{O}_Y)=0\). Now \(Y = Y'-\{x\}\) is an open set of \(Y'\), one therefore has an exact sequence (I (27)):

\[ \operatorname{H}^1(Y', \mathscr{O}_{Y'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^1(Y, \mathscr{O}_{Y})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^2_x(Y', \mathscr{O}_{Y'}){}, \]

whose right-hand term is zero by virtue of hypothesis b), and whose left-hand term is zero because \(Y'\) is affine.Q.E.D.

Lemma 3.17

Keeping the hypotheses of 3.16, suppose moreover that:

  • c)

    for every \(y\) closed in \(Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\geqslant 3\),

  • d)

    \(\operatorname{prof} A/tA\geqslant 4\) (stronger than b),

  • e)

    for every \(y\) closed in \(X\), \(y\in Y\), the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\) is parafactorial.

Then the map \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is an isomorphism, in particular in order that \(A\) be parafactorial, it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be so.

5mm One knows (X 2.1) that a) and c) entail \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\). Moreover, by the reasoning that has just been made, d) entails that \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, P_n)=0\) for every \(n\geqslant 0\) and \(i=1\) or \(i=2\). Moreover, for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), the complement of \(U\) in \(X\) consists of a finite number of closed points. Thanks to e) and to theorem 3.12, one deduces from this that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is an isomorphism. On the other hand \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant \operatorname{prof} B\geqslant 2\); by the criterion 3.5 one deduces from this that \(A\) is parafactorial if and only if \(B\) is so.

Let us now prove 3.13 (ii). Let \(R\) be a regular noetherian local ring. Let \((t_1, \ldots, t_k)\) be an \(R\)-sequence. Set \(B=R/(t_1, \ldots, t_k)\) and suppose that \(\dim B\geqslant 4\). One must prove that \(B\) is parafactorial. Let us argue by induction on \(k\). If \(k=0\), \(B\) is regular, hence factorial by 3.13 (i), hence parafactorial by 3.10. Suppose \(k\geqslant 1\) and the theorem proved for \(k'<k\). Set \(A=R/(t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1})\) hence \(B=A/t_k A\). One may suppose \(B\) complete by 3.7. By the induction hypothesis, \(A\) is parafactorial. Let us prove that one can apply lemma 3.17. One has supposed \(B\) complete hence also \(A\), and hence \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology. If \(x\in X\), and if \(x\) is closed in \(X\), \(A_x\) is a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 4\), with \(k'<k\). By the induction hypothesis \(A_x\) is parafactorial, and moreover of depth \(\geqslant 4\). This gives a), c) and e). Moreover, \(\dim A\geqslant 5\), whence d).Q.E.D.

Theorem 3.18

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(\mathscr{I}\) be a coherent sheaf of ideals of \(X\). Set \(Y=V(\mathscr{I})\). Let \(n\) be an integer. Suppose that:

  1. one has \(\operatorname{\ell eff}(X, Y)\) (cf. examples X 2.1 and X 2.2),

  2. for every \(p\geqslant n\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i(Y, \mathscr{I}^{p+1}/\mathscr{I}^{p+2})=0\) for \(i=1\) and \(i=2\),

  3. for every open set \(U\supset Y\) and every \(x\in X-U\), the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) be regular of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) or a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 4\).

Then, for every open set \(U\supset Y\) and every integer \(p\geqslant n\), the homomorphisms

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y_p) \]

are isomorphisms, where \(Y_p\) denotes the prescheme \((Y, \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{p+1})\).

It suffices to combine 3.12 and 3.13.

blocks.html: 3 Comparison of \(\mathbf{P}(X)\) and \(\mathbf{P}(U)\)
A definition:

blocks.title: Definition 3.1
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let \(X\) be a prescheme and let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Set \(U=X-Z\). One says that \(X\) is parafactorial at the points of \(Z\) if, for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the functor \(\mathbf{P}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}(V\cap U)\) is an equivalence of categories. One also says that the pair \((X, Z)\) is parafactorial.
Recall that \(\mathbf{P}(Z)\) denotes the category of locally free Modules of rank \(1\) on \(Z\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2

A noetherian local ring is said to be parafactorial if the pair \((\operatorname{Spec}(A), \{\mathfrak{r}(A)\})\) is parafactorial.
One proves the following proposition, which shows that the notion is "pointwise":

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3

Suppose \(X\) locally noetherian. In order that the pair \((X, Z)\) be parafactorial, it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(z\in Z\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, z}\) be so.
Let us note that within parafactorial there is "fully faithful". One proves, as in lemma 3.5 of exposé X, the:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4

If \(X\) is a locally noetherian prescheme and if \(Z=X-U\) is a closed subset of \(X\), the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the functor \(\mathbf{P}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}(V\cap U)\) is fully faithful;

  2. the homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*(\mathscr{O}_U)\) is an isomorphism;

  3. for every \(z\in Z\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}(\mathscr{O}_{X, z})\geqslant 2\).

Thus "parafactorial" means that the conditions of 3.4 are satisfied and that, for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the homomorphism \(\operatorname{Pic}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(V\cap U)\), is surjective. In particular, if \(X\) is the spectrum of a noetherian local ring, one finds:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; in order for it to be parafactorial it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(X'-\{x\})=0\), where one has set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and where \(x\) is the unique closed point of \(X'\).

One will note that a local ring of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) is never parafactorial, since its depth is \(\leqslant 1\). Hence factorial does not entail parafactorial; this is however true for noetherian local rings of dimension \(\geqslant 2\), as we shall see below.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.6

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(f\colon X_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Set \(Z_1=f^{-1}(Z)\). If \((X_1, Z_1)\) is parafactorial, then \((X, Z)\) is so.

One first remarks that, if \(i\colon(X-Z)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) denotes the canonical immersion of \(U=X-Z\) into \(X\), the formation of the direct image by \(i\) of a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module commutes with the base change \(f\), since the latter is flat. It is therefore equivalent to assume the equivalent conditions of lemma 3.5 for \((X, Z)\) or for \((X_1, Z_1)\), since \(f\) is a descent morphism for the category of quasi-coherent sheaves. It remains to prove that, for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), \(\operatorname{Pic}(V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(V\cap U)\) is surjective. One performs the base change \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), which changes nothing (sic), and one is reduced to \(V=X\). One then remarks that, if \({L}\) is an invertible \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module and if \({L}\) admits a locally free embedding, this prolongation is isomorphic to \(i_*({L})\), because of what has just been seen. It remains to prove that \(i_*({L})\) is invertible. Using again the fact that the direct image by \(i\) commutes with flat base change, and that "locally free of rank \(1\)" is a property that descends by faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, we have won.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; if \(\widehat{A}\) is parafactorial then \(A\) is so.
Do not believe that, if \(A\) is parafactorial, \(\widehat{A}\) is so.
!
Before approaching the statement of the principal theorem of this , let us make the link with the theory of divisors and the notion of factorial ring1.

Let \(X\) be a noetherian and normal prescheme. Let \(Z^1(X)\) be the free abelian group generated by the \(x\in X\) such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}=1\). The local ring of such a point is a discrete valuation ring. One will denote by \(v_x\) the corresponding normalized valuation. Let \(K(X)\) be the ring of rational fractions of \(X\) and let
\[ p\colon K(X)^*\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^1(X) \]
be the map which to every \(f\in K(X)^*\) associates the codimension-1 cycle:
\[ (f) = \sum_{x\in X, \;\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}=1} v_x(f)\cdot x{}. \]
The image of \(p\) is denoted \(P(X)\) and one calls its elements principal divisors2. One sets
\[ \operatorname{Cl}(X) = Z^1(X)/P(X){}. \]
Let \(Z\) be the subgroup of \(Z^1(X)\) whose elements are the locally principal divisors. One knows that
\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\simeq Z^{\prime1}(X)/P(X){}, \]
and consequently \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\) is identified with a subgroup of \(\operatorname{Cl}(X)\).

Let us remark that if \(U\) is a dense open set of \(X\), \(K(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K(U)\) is an isomorphism, and that if \(\operatorname{codim}(X-U, X)\geqslant 2\), i.e. if every \(x\in X\) such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\leqslant 1\) belongs to \(U\), the homomorphism \(Z^1(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^1(U)\) and consequently \(\operatorname{Cl}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Cl}(U)\) is also an isomorphism. Finally, if every \(x\in U\) is factorial, i.e. \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is so, then \(Z^1(U) = Z^{\prime1}(U)\) hence \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\simeq\operatorname{Cl}(U)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7.1

Let \(X\) be a noetherian and normal prescheme. Let \((U_i)_{i\in I}\) be a family of open sets of \(X\) such that:

  1. the \(U_i\) form a filter base;

  2. if one sets \(Y_i=X-U_i\), one has \(\operatorname{codim}(Y_i, X)\geqslant 2\) for every \(i\),

  3. if \(x\in U_i\) for every \(i\in I\), then \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is factorial.

Then one has an isomorphism:

\[ \varinjlim_{i\in I}\operatorname{Pic}(U_i)\xrightarrow{\approx}\operatorname{Cl}(X){}. \]

Let us remark that b) entails that every \(x\in X\) such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\leqslant 1\) belongs to \(U_i\) for every \(i\). Hence the \(U_i\) are dense and moreover the homomorphism \(Z^1(U_i)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z^1(X)\) is an isomorphism, as is \(K(U_i)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K(X)\). Hence \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)\subset \operatorname{Cl}(U_i) \simeq \operatorname{Cl}(X)\). To prove what one desires, it therefore suffices to show that every \(D\in Z^1(X)\) belongs to \(Z^{\prime1}(U_i)\) for a suitable \(i\). It suffices to do it for the irreducible and positive "divisors". So let \(x\in X\) be such that \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{X,x}=1\). It suffices to prove that there exists an \(i\in I\) such that \(\overline{\{x\}}\) is locally principal at the points of \(U_i\). Let \(\mathscr{I}\) be the largest ideal of definition of the closed set \(\overline{\{x\}}\). The set of points in a neighbourhood of which \(\mathscr{I}\) is free is an open set \(U\). Now \(U\supset \bigcap_{i\in I}U_i\) by c). If one sets \(Y=X-U\), one has \(Y\subset \bigcup_{i\in I}Y_i\), now \(Y\) is closed, hence admits a finite number of generic points, hence is contained in the union of a finite number of \(Y_i\), hence in \(Y_j\) for a \(j\in I\), since the \(U_i\) form a filter base. Hence \(U\supset U_j\).Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let \(X\) be a noetherian and normal prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed subset of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Suppose that, for every \(p\in X-Y\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X, p}\) is factorial, then

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X-Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Cl}(X-Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Cl}(X) \]

are isomorphisms.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.9

Let \(A\) be a noetherian and normal local ring. Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(x = \mathfrak{r}(A)\). In order that \(A\) be factorial it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X'-\{x\})=0\) and that \(\mathfrak{p}\in X'-\{x\}\) imply that \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) is factorial.
Indeed, in order that \(A\) be factorial it is necessary and sufficient that \(\operatorname{Cl}(X')=0\) .

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.10

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 2\). Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and let \(x = \mathfrak{r}(A)\). Set \(X=X'-\{x\}\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(A\) is factorial;

  2. a) for every \(y\in X\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X, p}\) is factorial, and

    6.3mmb) \(A\) is parafactorial, i.e. \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=0\).
Before proving this corollary, let us state the

blocks.title: Serre's normality criterion 3.11
blocks.html:
Serre's normality criterion 3.11

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. In order that \(A\) be normal, it is necessary and sufficient that

  1. for every prime ideal \({\mathfrak{p}}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\leqslant 1\), \(A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\) be normal,

  2. for every prime ideal \({\mathfrak{p}}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\geqslant 2\), one have \(\operatorname{prof} A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\geqslant 2\).
Let us prove 3.10.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Knowing that a localization of a factorial ring is also factorial, one has (ii) a). Moreover \(A\) is normal hence \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) since \(\dim A\geqslant 2\) (3.11 (ii)). Finally \(A\) is parafactorial; indeed \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\simeq\operatorname{Cl}(X')=0\) (cf. 3.9).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). Let us first prove that \(A\) is normal by applying SERRE's criterion. Since \(\dim A\geqslant 2\), condition (i) of the criterion is among the hypotheses. Moreover, for every \({\mathfrak{p}}\in X\), \(A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\) is factorial, hence normal, hence of depth \(\geqslant 2\), at least if \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\geqslant 2\). Finally \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\) by (ii) b). It remains to apply 3.9.
Let us summarize what precedes:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.12

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(\mathscr{I}\) be a quasi-coherent ideal of \(X\). Let \(Y=V(\mathscr{I})\). Let \(p\in\mathbf{N}\). Suppose that:

  1. 1)

    One has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\) (Exposé X);

  2. 2)

    \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, \mathscr{I}^{n+1}/\mathscr{I}^{n+2})=0\) if \(i=1\) or \(2\) and if \(n\geqslant p\);

  3. 3)

    for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\) and every \(x\in X-U\), the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) be parafactorial.

Then, for every \(n\geqslant p\), and every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\), the homomorphisms

\[ \xymatrix{{\mathrm{Pic}(X)}\ar[rr]\ar[dr]&&{\mathrm{Pic}(X_n)}\\&{\mathrm{Pic}(U)}\ar[ur]&} \]

are isomorphisms.
One knows of some parafactorial rings:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.13

  1. (Auslander-Buchsbaum) A regular noetherian local ring is factorial (hence parafactorial if its dimension is \(\geqslant 2\)).

  2. A noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 4\) which is a complete intersection is parafactorial.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary (SAMUEL's conjecture)

A noetherian local ring \(A\) which is a complete intersection and which is factorial in codimension \(\geqslant 3\) (i.e. \(\dim A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\leqslant 3\) entails that \(A_{{\mathfrak{p}}}\) is factorial) is factorial.
Let us prove the corollary
One argues by induction on the dimension of \(A\).
If \(\dim A\leqslant 3\), \(A\) is factorial by hypothesis.

If \(\dim A>3\), by the induction hypothesis, remarking that a localization of a complete intersection is also one, all the localizations of \(A\) other than \(A\) are factorial. By theorem 3.13 (ii), \(A\) is parafactorial, hence factorial by 3.10.
Let us prove 3.13 (i) (following KAPLANSKY)3.

Let \(A\) be a regular noetherian local ring, set \(\dim A=n\). If \(n=0\) or \(1\), the result is known. Suppose \(n\geqslant 2\), let us argue by induction on \(n\) and suppose \(n\geqslant 2\) and the theorem proved for rings of dimension \(<n\). Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(X=X'-\{x\}\), where \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\). The localizations of \(A\) other than \(A\) are regular and of dimension \(<n\), hence factorial. Moreover \(\operatorname{prof} A = \dim A\geqslant 2\). It therefore suffices to prove that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=0\) (cor. 3.10). So let \({L}\) be an invertible \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module; one knows that it can be prolonged to a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Module \({L}'\). There exists a resolution of \({L}'\) by free \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}'\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}'_1\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}\cdots\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{L}'_n\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} 0{}, \]
since the cohomological dimension of \(A\) is finite. By restriction to \(X'\) one obtains a finite free resolution. It therefore suffices to prove the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.15

Let \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) be a ringed space and let \({L}\) be a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module which admits a finite resolution by free modules of finite type. Then \(\det({L}) \simeq \mathscr{O}_X\).

Recall that one defines \(\det({L})\) as the maximal exterior power of \({L}\). In the case considered, \(\det({L}) \simeq{L}\) since \({L}\) is invertible, hence the lemma allows one to conclude. Let us prove this lemma. Let

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}_0\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}_1\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} {L}_2\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}\cdots\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{L}_n\mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} 0{}, \]
be the announced exact sequence, where \({L}_0={L}\). Since everything is locally free, one has:
\[ \mathop{\textstyle\bigotimes}\limits_{0\leqslant i\leqslant n}(\det({L}_i))^{(-1)^i} \simeq \mathscr{O}_X \]
now all the \({L}_i\), \(i>0\), are free, hence so are their determinants, hence so is that of \({L}_0 = {L}\).Q.E.D.
It remains for us to prove part (ii) of the theorem. Beforehand, let us prove a lemma which will allow us to proceed by induction:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.16

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring that is a quotient of a regular one. Let \(t\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be an \(A\)-regular element. Suppose that \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology. Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(Y'=V(t)\simeq \operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(B=A/tA\), \(X=X'-\{x\}\), \(Y=Y'-\{x\}\), \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\). Suppose that:

  1. for every \(y\in X\) closed in \(X\) one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\geqslant 2\),

  2. \(\operatorname{prof} A/tA\geqslant 3\),

then the map \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is injective. In particular, if \(B\) is parafactorial, \(A\) is so too.

One knows that a) entails \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\) thanks to X 2.1. If one proves that \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, P_n)=0\) for every \(n\geqslant 0\), one will know (2.2) that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is injective. If, moreover, \(B\) is parafactorial, one will know that \(\operatorname{Pic}(Y)=0\) (3.5) hence \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)=0\), now \(\operatorname{prof}(A)=3+1\geqslant 2\) since \(t\) is \(A\)-regular, hence \(A\) will be parafactorial by 3.5.

Let \(\mathscr{I}=\widetilde{(tA)}\) be the \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Module associated with the ideal \(tA\). In n° 1, one set \(P_n = (\mathscr{I}^{n+1}/\mathscr{I}^{n+2}){|Y}\), for every \(n\geqslant 0\). Now \(t\) is \(A\)-regular hence \(P_n\simeq \mathscr{O}_Y\). It therefore remains for us to prove that \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, \mathscr{O}_Y)=0\). Now \(Y = Y'-\{x\}\) is an open set of \(Y'\), one therefore has an exact sequence (I (27)):

\[ \operatorname{H}^1(Y', \mathscr{O}_{Y'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^1(Y, \mathscr{O}_{Y})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^2_x(Y', \mathscr{O}_{Y'}){}, \]
whose right-hand term is zero by virtue of hypothesis b), and whose left-hand term is zero because \(Y'\) is affine.Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.17

Keeping the hypotheses of 3.16, suppose moreover that:

  • c)

    for every \(y\) closed in \(Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\geqslant 3\),

  • d)

    \(\operatorname{prof} A/tA\geqslant 4\) (stronger than b),

  • e)

    for every \(y\) closed in \(X\), \(y\in Y\), the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\) is parafactorial.

Then the map \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is an isomorphism, in particular in order that \(A\) be parafactorial, it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be so.

5mm One knows (X 2.1) that a) and c) entail \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\). Moreover, by the reasoning that has just been made, d) entails that \(\operatorname{H}^1(Y, P_n)=0\) for every \(n\geqslant 0\) and \(i=1\) or \(i=2\). Moreover, for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), the complement of \(U\) in \(X\) consists of a finite number of closed points. Thanks to e) and to theorem 3.12, one deduces from this that \(\operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}(Y)\) is an isomorphism. On the other hand \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant \operatorname{prof} B\geqslant 2\); by the criterion 3.5 one deduces from this that \(A\) is parafactorial if and only if \(B\) is so.

Let us now prove 3.13 (ii). Let \(R\) be a regular noetherian local ring. Let \((t_1, \ldots, t_k)\) be an \(R\)-sequence. Set \(B=R/(t_1, \ldots, t_k)\) and suppose that \(\dim B\geqslant 4\). One must prove that \(B\) is parafactorial. Let us argue by induction on \(k\). If \(k=0\), \(B\) is regular, hence factorial by 3.13 (i), hence parafactorial by 3.10. Suppose \(k\geqslant 1\) and the theorem proved for \(k'<k\). Set \(A=R/(t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1})\) hence \(B=A/t_k A\). One may suppose \(B\) complete by 3.7. By the induction hypothesis, \(A\) is parafactorial. Let us prove that one can apply lemma 3.17. One has supposed \(B\) complete hence also \(A\), and hence \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology. If \(x\in X\), and if \(x\) is closed in \(X\), \(A_x\) is a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 4\), with \(k'<k\). By the induction hypothesis \(A_x\) is parafactorial, and moreover of depth \(\geqslant 4\). This gives a), c) and e). Moreover, \(\dim A\geqslant 5\), whence d).Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.18

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(\mathscr{I}\) be a coherent sheaf of ideals of \(X\). Set \(Y=V(\mathscr{I})\). Let \(n\) be an integer. Suppose that:

  1. one has \(\operatorname{\ell eff}(X, Y)\) (cf. examples X 2.1 and X 2.2),

  2. for every \(p\geqslant n\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^i(Y, \mathscr{I}^{p+1}/\mathscr{I}^{p+2})=0\) for \(i=1\) and \(i=2\),

  3. for every open set \(U\supset Y\) and every \(x\in X-U\), the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) be regular of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) or a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant 4\).

Then, for every open set \(U\supset Y\) and every integer \(p\geqslant n\), the homomorphisms

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Pic}(Y_p) \]

are isomorphisms, where \(Y_p\) denotes the prescheme \((Y, \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{p+1})\).
It suffices to combine 3.12 and 3.13.

footnotes.html: For the generalities that follow, cf. also EGA IV 21.
In accordance with the terminology of EGA IV 21, we now prefer to reserve the name "divisors" for the "locally principal divisors" or "Cartier divisors".
This is the proof reproduced in EGA IV 21.11.1.
